A lock-free ring buffer manages wraparound by treating the underlying array as a circular space where indices are incremented modulo the buffer size, ensuring that logical continuity is maintained despite physical array boundaries. To handle concurrent access without locks, the implementation relies on atomic compare-and-swap operations to update head and tail pointers, guaranteeing that only one thread successfully claims a slot or consumes data at a time. Memory ordering is critical here; the producer must ensure that data is fully written to the buffer before publishing the new tail index to the consumer, typically using release semantics on the store operation, while the consumer must read the data before updating the head index,
